heroOverview: Cholangitis is a bacterial infection of the bile ducts — the tubes that carry digestive bile from the liver and gallbladder to the small intestine — almost always triggered by obstruction that allows bacteria to proliferate in stagnant bile and back-pressure forces them into the bloodstream. The classic presentation, Charcot's triad of right-upper-quadrant pain, jaundice, and fever, appears in roughly 50-70% of cases; when hypotension and confusion are added (Reynolds' pentad) the mortality risk climbs sharply. Cholangitis accounts for an estimated 1-3% of all acute abdominal emergencies presenting to hospital, and gallstones obstructing the common bile duct are responsible for about 80% of cases. Prompt biliary decompression — usually by endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) within 24-72 hours — combined with broad-spectrum antibiotics has reduced the once-substantial mortality of severe cholangitis from over 50% to under 5% in modern series.

keyStats.prevalence: Accounts for 1-3% of acute abdominal emergencies; the annual incidence in the United States is estimated at 4-8 cases per 100,000 population
keyStats.demographics: Affects adults of all ages but most commonly those aged 50-70; slight male predominance for choledocholithiasis-related cases
keyStats.avgAge: Mean presentation age approximately 55-65 years in Western populations
keyStats.globalCases: Precise global figures are not reported uniformly; biliary-stone disease affects roughly 10-15% of adults in high-income countries, making cholangitis a common downstream complication

definition: Cholangitis (ICD-10: K83.0), also called ascending cholangitis or acute bacterial cholangitis, is inflammation of the biliary ductal system caused by bacterial infection superimposed on biliary stasis or obstruction. Normally, bile is sterile and the sphincter of Oddi at the duodenum limits reflux of intestinal bacteria; when obstruction raises intraductal pressure above roughly 25 cmH₂O, bacteria — chiefly Escherichia coli, Klebsiella species, and Enterococcus — ascend from the duodenum or translocate through the portal circulation and colonise the stagnant bile. The resulting infection triggers a systemic inflammatory response that in severe cases progresses to septic shock and multi-organ dysfunction. Cholangitis falls under the gastroenterology specialty, with hepatology and interventional gastroenterology (ERCP) central to management; hepatobiliary surgery is involved when endoscopic or percutaneous drainage fails. The 2018 Tokyo Guidelines (TG18) classify severity into grade I (mild, antibiotic-responsive), grade II (moderate, requiring drainage within 24 hours), and grade III (severe and septic, requiring drainage within 6-12 hours) to stratify urgency and guide treatment decisions.

typesClassification.description: The most common type, occurring when a common bile duct stone obstructs bile flow; accounts for approximately 80% of all cases.
Develops after ERCP, biliary stenting, or liver biopsy when instrumentation introduces bacteria or incomplete drainage leaves stagnant bile.
Cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic head carcinoma, or ampullary tumours obstruct the duct; drainage is palliative but controls sepsis.

typesClassification.type: Primary sclerosing cholangitis with superimposed infection
typesClassification.description: Patients with PSC are at heightened risk of recurrent cholangitis due to structurally abnormal, strictured ducts that pool bile.
A distinct entity prevalent in East Asia, characterised by repeated episodes from biliary stones formed within the intrahepatic ducts, often associated with parasitic infection.

primarySymptoms: Right upper quadrant or epigastric abdominal pain — present in roughly 70-80% of cases — caused by distension and inflammation of the bile ducts.
Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and white of the eyes), which signals bile flow obstruction and bilirubin spilling into the circulation.
Fever above 38°C, often with rigors (shaking chills), reflecting bacteraemia as bacteria enter the bloodstream from obstructed ducts.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying the abdominal pain and systemic inflammatory response.
Dark, tea-coloured urine due to conjugated bilirubin excreted by the kidneys when bile cannot drain normally.
Pale or clay-coloured stools resulting from bile pigment being absent from the gastrointestinal tract.
Confusion, altered level of consciousness, or frank delirium in severe cases (Reynolds' pentad with hypotension), signalling septic shock or hepatic encephalopathy.
Hypotension and rapid heart rate when infection becomes systemic, indicating progression to sepsis requiring urgent intensive care.
Generalised fatigue and malaise beginning 24-48 hours before localised signs become apparent.
Pruritus (skin itching) in patients with prolonged obstruction, caused by bile-salt accumulation in the skin.
earlyWarningSigns: Mild right-sided upper abdominal aching starting hours before fever develops.
Urine darkening subtly one to two days before jaundice becomes visible.
A single rigor (shaking chill) with mild fever in a patient known to have gallstones.
Unexplained fatigue and loss of appetite in the 48 hours before biliary colic.
emergencySigns: Fever with rigors plus jaundice and right upper quadrant pain together — Charcot's triad — requires same-day emergency assessment.
Hypotension (systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg) or confusion added to Charcot's triad constitutes Reynolds' pentad — a life-threatening emergency.
Signs of septic shock: a heart rate above 100 beats per minute, rapid breathing, confusion, or mottled skin with fever.
Progressive confusion or drowsiness in a jaundiced patient, which can indicate septic hepatic dysfunction.
Failure to improve within 12-24 hours of antibiotics, indicating the need for urgent biliary decompression.

causes.description: Gallstones migrating from the gallbladder into the common bile duct block bile outflow, raise intraductal pressure, and allow intestinal bacteria to colonise the stagnant bile pool; this mechanism accounts for roughly 80% of cholangitis cases in Western populations.

Benign strictures from post-operative scarring, primary sclerosing cholangitis, or chronic pancreatitis narrow the duct lumen and create a zone of stasis upstream where bacteria proliferate; the reduced diameter also limits natural bile flushing.

Tumours of the bile duct (cholangiocarcinoma), pancreatic head, or ampulla of Vater compress or invade the duct, producing the same obstructive-infective mechanism as stones; infection is often a complication of stenting rather than of the tumour itself.

Endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography introduces duodenal bacteria directly into the biliary tree; inadequate drainage after the procedure leaves inoculated bile stagnating, and the risk is highest when the duct cannot be fully cleared in a single session.

Clonorchis sinensis, Opisthorchis, and Ascaris lumbricoides can obstruct or colonise the bile ducts, a mechanism responsible for recurrent pyogenic cholangitis predominantly in Southeast and East Asia and in immigrants from endemic regions.

Plastic and, to a lesser extent, metal stents placed to relieve obstruction can become occluded with biofilm, bile sludge, or food debris over weeks to months, converting a patent stent into a new source of obstruction and infection requiring stent exchange.

riskFactors.description: Cholelithiasis is the dominant risk factor; approximately 10-15% of adults in Western countries have gallstones, and bile duct stones develop in 10-15% of them, providing the substrate for most cholangitis episodes.
Surgical anastomoses, sphincterotomy, and prior stent placement alter biliary anatomy and the resident microbiome, increasing susceptibility to ascending infection in subsequent episodes.

PSC causes progressive stricturing of intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts, creating multiple zones of stasis; over half of PSC patients experience at least one episode of bacterial cholangitis during their disease course.

riskFactors.factor: Biliary stent or prosthesis in situ
riskFactors.description: Plastic biliary stents occlude at a median of 3-6 months; timely elective stent exchange before occlusion substantially reduces cholangitis risk.
Adults over 65 have a higher prevalence of gallstones, reduced immune response, and more frequent biliary interventions, contributing to a disproportionate share of severe cholangitis cases.
Reduced immune function allows opportunistic biliary pathogens such as Cryptosporidium and cytomegalovirus to cause AIDS cholangiopathy, a form of chronic biliary damage with obstructive features.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to parasite-endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Exposure to liver flukes (Clonorchis, Opisthorchis) through consumption of raw freshwater fish is an established cause of recurrent pyogenic cholangitis in East and Southeast Asia.
Choledochal cysts, biliary atresia repairs, and Caroli disease create abnormal bile duct anatomy with poor drainage and chronic stone formation.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 50-70 as the peak population for gallstone-related cholangitis
Patients with known gallstone disease or prior cholecystectomy
People with indwelling biliary stents for benign or malignant obstruction
Patients with primary sclerosing cholangitis, often younger adults with IBD
Individuals from or travelling to East and Southeast Asia who may have liver-fluke infection
Immunocompromised individuals including transplant recipients and those with HIV
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical findings, blood tests, and imaging. The Tokyo Guidelines criteria require two of three elements: systemic inflammation (fever, elevated white cell count or CRP), cholestasis (elevated bilirubin or alkaline phosphatase), and evidence of biliary dilatation or obstruction on imaging. Blood tests typically show elevated bilirubin (usually above 34 µmol/L), raised alkaline phosphatase and gamma-GT, and a neutrophilia with elevated CRP or procalcitonin; blood cultures are positive in 40-60% of cases and guide antibiotic selection. Right upper quadrant ultrasound is the first-line imaging modality, identifying bile duct dilatation (common duct diameter above 6 mm is a useful threshold) and gallstones with high specificity, though it misses duct stones in up to 30% of cases because overlying bowel gas obscures the distal duct. Magnetic resonance cholangiopancreatography (MRCP) is more accurate for distal stones and strictures and is preferred when the diagnosis is uncertain, since it is non-invasive and avoids radiation. CT abdomen clarifies anatomy when malignancy or complications such as liver abscess are suspected. ERCP is simultaneously diagnostic and therapeutic — it directly visualises the duct, retrieves stones, and provides tissue samples from strictures — and is indicated once the diagnosis is established and the patient is stable enough to undergo the procedure.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms cholestasis pattern (elevated bilirubin, ALP, GGT) and systemic inflammation (elevated WBC, CRP, procalcitonin); LFTs also gauge degree of hepatic dysfunction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard arm blood draw with results typically available within 2-4 hours; the pattern of liver tests guides urgency of further investigation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities in the 40-60% of patients with bacteraemia, allowing targeted therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Two sets of blood are drawn from separate sites before antibiotics are started; culture results take 24-72 hours, but therapy begins empirically immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line imaging to detect bile duct dilatation, gallstones, and liver changes; quick, non-invasive, and available at the bedside.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A gel is applied to the upper abdomen and a probe moved across the skin; takes approximately 20-30 minutes with no radiation or preparation needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Non-invasive, high-resolution mapping of bile duct anatomy, stones, and strictures; preferred when ultrasound is inconclusive and surgery or malignancy is considered.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still in an MRI scanner for 30-45 minutes; no contrast injection or radiation, but the machine is noisy and claustrophobia is occasionally a problem.

diagnosticTests.test: CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects complications such as liver abscess, periductal oedema, or malignant duct obstruction; especially useful when severe disease or tumour is suspected.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An intravenous contrast dye is injected and you are scanned for 5-10 minutes; kidney function is checked beforehand.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly visualises the bile duct, removes stones or sludge, places stents, and collects bile for culture; the definitive diagnostic and therapeutic procedure.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under sedation or general anaesthesia; a flexible endoscope passes through the mouth to the duodenum where the bile duct opening is cannulated; takes 30-60 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies biliary pathogens directly, which can differ from blood culture isolates and refine antibiotic choice.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bile is aspirated during ERCP or percutaneous drainage and sent to the laboratory; results return in 24-72 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of cholangitis requires two simultaneous priorities: antibiotic therapy to control infection and biliary decompression to relieve the obstruction driving that infection. Antibiotics should be started within the first hour of diagnosis using a broad-spectrum regimen covering Gram-negative enteric bacteria and Enterococcus; typical empirical choices include piperacillin-tazobactam, a carbapenem, or a third-generation cephalosporin combined with metronidazole for anaerobes. The choice is narrowed once cultures return. The 2018 Tokyo Guidelines recommend biliary drainage within 24 hours for moderate (grade II) disease and within 6-12 hours for severe (grade III) septic cases; grade I cases are often treated with antibiotics alone initially, with elective drainage deferred. ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is the standard drainage method for choledocholithiasis, achieving successful decompression in over 90% of cases. When ERCP fails or is not feasible, percutaneous transhepatic cholangiodrainage (PTCD) provides an alternative route. Surgical common bile duct exploration is rarely needed as a primary intervention but may be required after failed endoscopic or percutaneous approaches. Following recovery from the acute episode, cholecystectomy is recommended during the same admission or within 4-6 weeks if gallstones were the cause, to prevent recurrence. Patients with malignant obstruction may receive palliative biliary stenting. ICU admission and organ support are required for grade III disease with septic shock.

medicalTreatments.name: Piperacillin-tazobactam (empirical antibiotic)
medicalTreatments.description: A first-line broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotic covering E. coli, Klebsiella, Pseudomonas, and Enterococcus; given every 6-8 hours and continued for 4-7 days once the patient is afebrile and the duct is decompressed.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves clinical cure in approximately 85-90% of moderate cholangitis cases when combined with biliary drainage.

medicalTreatments.name: Carbapenems (meropenem, imipenem)
medicalTreatments.description: Reserved for severe cases, hospital-acquired infection, or when local resistance patterns or prior cultures suggest resistant organisms; the broadest available intravenous cover.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical success rates exceed 90% in severe cholangitis when drainage is also achieved; failure usually reflects inadequate decompression rather than antibiotic resistance.

medicalTreatments.name: Ceftriaxone plus metronidazole
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative outpatient-capable regimen for mild-to-moderate cholangitis in a patient without risk factors for resistant organisms; ceftriaxone covers Gram-negatives and metronidazole adds anaerobic cover.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolution rates of approximately 80-85% in grade I-II disease with adequate drainage.

medicalTreatments.name: Ursodeoxycholic acid (UDCA)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in primary sclerosing cholangitis and after stone removal to improve bile flow, reduce bile viscosity, and lower the risk of stone reformation and subsequent cholangitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In PSC, UDCA improves biochemical markers in approximately 50-60% of patients, though it has not been shown to delay liver transplant.

medicalTreatments.name: ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive therapeutic procedure for choledocholithiasis; a cut is made at the bile duct opening and stones are pulled out with a basket or balloon, restoring bile flow.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful ductal clearance in more than 90% of cases in a single ERCP session; mortality of the cholangitis episode falls from over 25% (untreated) to under 5% with timely ERCP.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic cholecystectomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the gallbladder after recovery from the acute cholangitis episode to eliminate the stone source and prevent recurrence; ideally performed during the same hospital admission or within 4-6 weeks.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Prevents cholangitis recurrence in over 95% of patients when the gallbladder and duct are both cleared; complication rate below 2% in elective setting.

surgicalOptions.name: Open or laparoscopic common bile duct exploration
surgicalOptions.description: Direct surgical exploration and stone removal from the common bile duct, now rarely needed as a primary procedure but used when ERCP has failed or is anatomically impossible (e.g., after Roux-en-Y gastric bypass).
surgicalOptions.successRate: Duct clearance achieved in approximately 90-95% of cases; procedure-related mortality under 2% in elective settings.

surgicalOptions.name: Percutaneous transhepatic cholangiodrainage (PTCD)
surgicalOptions.description: A radiologically guided needle passed through the liver to drain the obstructed duct from above when endoscopic access is impossible; can be a bridge to definitive treatment or a long-term palliative measure.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Technical success in draining the bile duct is achieved in 85-95% of cases; complication rates are higher than ERCP, including bleeding and bile leak in 5-10%.

surgicalOptions.name: Hepaticojejunostomy (biliary bypass surgery)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical reconstruction joining the bile duct to the small bowel, used for definitive management of benign biliary strictures or as bypass in unresectable malignant obstruction.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term patency rates of 80-90% at 5 years for benign stricture repair; recurrent cholangitis risk is substantially reduced.

alternativeTreatments.name: Endoscopic ultrasound-guided biliary drainage (EUS-BD)
alternativeTreatments.description: A newer endoscopic technique that creates a fistula between the gut wall and bile duct under ultrasound guidance, offering drainage when conventional ERCP fails; performed at specialist centres.

alternativeTreatments.name: Antiparasitic therapy for fluke-related cholangitis
alternativeTreatments.description: Praziquantel or albendazole treat Clonorchis or Opisthorchis infestation; eliminating the parasite removes a chronic source of biliary inflammation and stone formation in endemic-region patients.

whySeeSpecialist: Cholangitis is a gastroenterological and hepatobiliary emergency; a gastroenterologist or hepatologist with ERCP capability should be involved in all but the mildest cases. Specialist intervention is required for biliary decompression, culture-directed antibiotic therapy, and management of underlying cause — whether stone disease, stricture, or malignancy. Patients with recurrent episodes, primary sclerosing cholangitis, or suspected malignancy need multidisciplinary input including hepatology, interventional radiology, and hepatobiliary surgery.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a gastroenterologist or hepatobiliary surgeon with high-volume ERCP experience — institutions performing more than 200 ERCPs annually have lower complication rates. For PSC-related cholangitis, a hepatologist with a dedicated IBD-liver clinic provides integrated care. If malignant obstruction is suspected, a hepatopancreaticobiliary (HPB) team with on-site interventional radiology and oncology is essential.
preventionStrategies: Undergo elective cholecystectomy after a confirmed first episode of cholangitis caused by gallstones to prevent recurrence.
Arrange timely elective replacement of plastic biliary stents at 3-6 months before they occlude, reducing infection risk.
In PSC, maintain regular surveillance endoscopy and MRCP to detect and treat dominant strictures before infection supervenes.
Prophylactic antibiotics (e.g., ciprofloxacin) before ERCP are recommended for patients with known biliary obstruction or high infection risk.
In regions where liver flukes are endemic, avoid raw or undercooked freshwater fish to reduce fluke-related biliary disease.
Control underlying metabolic risk factors for gallstone formation, including obesity and rapid weight loss.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain a healthy weight; obesity is a major risk factor for gallstone disease, the primary cause of cholangitis.
Avoid prolonged fasting or very-low-calorie diets, which accelerate gallstone formation.
In patients with known PSC, avoid alcohol entirely as it accelerates hepatic fibrosis.
Comply with all follow-up appointments for stent surveillance and biliary imaging.
Report new fever, jaundice, or abdominal pain promptly — early recognition of recurrence improves outcomes.

recommended: Low-fat meals to reduce biliary stimulation during the recovery phase
Adequate hydration to support bile flow and kidney function during antibiotic therapy
Regular, moderate fat intake long-term to maintain gallbladder contractility and reduce bile stasis
Fibre-rich foods that promote regular bowel function and reduce enterohepatic bacterial translocation
avoid: Large high-fat meals immediately after recovery, which strongly stimulate bile flow and can precipitate biliary pain
Alcohol, particularly in PSC or any liver-related cholangitis
Prolonged fasting and very-low-calorie diets that favour gallstone nucleation
Raw freshwater fish in parasite-endemic regions

exerciseGuidelines: During the acute episode, bed rest is necessary. Once the infection has been controlled and biliary drainage achieved, gentle walking can begin within a few days. A return to full activity is appropriate over two to four weeks for uncomplicated cases. Patients recovering from laparoscopic cholecystectomy can usually return to light exercise within one to two weeks and full activity by four weeks.
dailyManagement: Complete the full antibiotic course as prescribed, typically 4-7 days after decompression.
Monitor temperature twice daily during recovery and seek immediate review if fever recurs.
Attend scheduled follow-up imaging and stent replacement appointments without delay.
Report new jaundice, abdominal pain, or rigors immediately — these are signs of recurrent cholangitis.
If on ursodeoxycholic acid for PSC, take it consistently and attend regular liver function monitoring.
prognosis: With timely biliary drainage and appropriate antibiotics, the prognosis for mild-to-moderate cholangitis is excellent — in-hospital mortality is below 3% for grade I-II disease managed per current guidelines. Severe cholangitis with septic shock (grade III) carries higher risk, with mortality in the range of 5-10% even with optimal care, rising further in elderly or immunocompromised patients with delayed presentation. Recurrence is the main long-term concern: if the underlying cause is not corrected — most importantly by cholecystectomy after stone-related episodes — roughly 20-30% of patients have a recurrence within one year. Patients with PSC or malignant obstruction face a less predictable course governed by the progression of their underlying disease rather than the infection itself. When cholangitis is treated promptly and the causative obstruction addressed, most patients recover full liver function without permanent hepatic damage.
recoveryTimeline: Fever typically resolves within 24-72 hours of effective biliary drainage and antibiotics. Jaundice fades over three to seven days as bilirubin clears, and liver test abnormalities normalise over two to four weeks. Patients treated with ERCP alone are commonly discharged within three to five days; those requiring surgery or intensive care may need one to three weeks in hospital. Full return to normal activities takes two to four weeks for uncomplicated cases. Patients with ongoing biliary stents or PSC require indefinite surveillance.
complications: Septic shock — bacteria flood the bloodstream and cause multi-organ failure; the most dangerous acute complication, occurring in roughly 10-20% of severe presentations.
Liver abscess — a pocket of infection develops in the liver parenchyma when ductal infection spreads locally; requires prolonged antibiotics and often percutaneous or surgical drainage.
Acute renal failure — a consequence of sepsis and hypotension; requires fluid resuscitation and, in severe cases, renal replacement therapy.
Acute pancreatitis — simultaneous inflammation of the pancreas occurs in approximately 5-10% of cholangitis cases when the common channel is obstructed.
Recurrent cholangitis — repeated episodes cause progressive biliary scarring and, in PSC patients, accelerate progression to biliary cirrhosis.
Secondary biliary cirrhosis — prolonged or recurrent obstruction leads to permanent hepatic fibrosis and eventually portal hypertension and liver failure.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Gallstones
supportResources.description: Patient overview of gallstone disease, the primary cause of cholangitis, from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: NHS — Acute cholangitis
supportResources.description: Plain-English UK guidance on bile duct infection symptoms, causes, and treatment.

supportResources.name: PSC Partners Seeking a Cure
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy and support organisation for primary sclerosing cholangitis, a common predisposing condition for recurrent cholangitis.

sources.title: Miura F et al. Tokyo Guidelines 2018: initial management of acute biliary infection and flowchart for acute cholangitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018
Kiriyama S et al. Tokyo Guidelines 2018: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholangitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018
ASGE Standards of Practice Committee — The role of endoscopy in the evaluation and management of choledocholithiasis. Gastrointest Endosc. 2019
Mosler P. Diagnosis and management of acute cholangitis. Curr Gastroenterol Rep. 2011
NICE — Gallstone disease: diagnosis and initial management. Clinical guideline CG188. 2014 (updated 2023)

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute cholecystitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cholecystitis is infection of the gallbladder wall producing right-upper-quadrant pain and fever but typically without jaundice; cholangitis involves the duct itself and usually causes visible jaundice and bilirubinuria.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute pancreatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pancreatitis causes epigastric pain radiating to the back with elevated lipase and amylase; it can coexist with cholangitis but lacks the distinctive jaundice-fever-pain triad of Charcot.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hepatitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Viral or drug-induced hepatitis can present with jaundice and malaise but without the obstructive pattern on liver tests or the duct dilatation on imaging seen in cholangitis.

coOccurringConditions.name: Common bile duct stone with cholangitis

relatedConditions.name: Acute cholecystitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the same gallstone aetiology and often coexists; cholecystitis confined to the gallbladder is a common precursor to duct stone migration and cholangitis.

relatedConditions.name: Biliary acute pancreatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the same impacted common bile duct stone trigger; pancreatitis and cholangitis may occur simultaneously or sequentially from a single obstructing stone.

relatedConditions.name: Alcoholic cirrhosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Liver disease alters biliary anatomy and immune function, increasing vulnerability to biliary infections and complicating management.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Inflammatory liver disease can mimic cholangitis biochemically and must be differentiated; the two conditions can also occur in the same patient.

faqs.question: What is cholangitis and how serious is it?
faqs.answer: Cholangitis is a bacterial infection of the bile ducts, almost always caused by an obstruction that allows bacteria to multiply in pooled bile and enter the bloodstream. Without prompt treatment it can progress to septic shock. With modern antibiotics and drainage procedures (usually ERCP), mortality is now below 3-5% for most cases.

faqs.question: What is Charcot's triad?
faqs.answer: Charcot's triad is the classic set of three signs seen in roughly 50-70% of cholangitis cases: right upper abdominal pain, jaundice, and fever with chills. When hypotension and confusion are also present the combination is called Reynolds' pentad and indicates severe septic cholangitis needing emergency drainage.

faqs.question: What causes cholangitis?
faqs.answer: In about 80% of cases in Western countries, a gallstone has migrated into the common bile duct and blocked bile flow, allowing intestinal bacteria to infect the stagnant bile. Other causes include biliary strictures, cancers blocking the duct, indwelling biliary stents that have become clogged, and liver flukes in parts of Asia.

faqs.question: How is cholangitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on the Tokyo Guidelines criteria: signs of infection (fever, raised white cell count), evidence of bile duct obstruction (raised bilirubin or liver enzymes), and duct dilatation or obstruction on ultrasound or MRCP. Blood cultures are taken before antibiotics start. ERCP both confirms and treats the cause in most cases.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Treatment has two pillars: broad-spectrum intravenous antibiotics started immediately, and biliary drainage to relieve the obstruction. ERCP with stone extraction is the standard drainage method for gallstone-related cases, achieving decompression in over 90% of patients. Cholecystectomy follows to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: How quickly does cholangitis need to be treated?
faqs.answer: Moderate cholangitis should be drained within 24 hours; severe cholangitis with signs of septic shock needs drainage within 6-12 hours. Antibiotics should start within the first hour of diagnosis. Delay significantly worsens outcomes — untreated severe cholangitis carries mortality above 50%.

faqs.question: Will I need surgery for cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Most patients do not need emergency surgery; ERCP clears bile duct stones endoscopically in over 90% of cases. After recovery, laparoscopic cholecystectomy (gallbladder removal) is recommended if gallstones were the cause. Surgical common bile duct exploration is reserved for the minority in whom ERCP fails or is anatomically impossible.

faqs.question: Can cholangitis come back?
faqs.answer: Without treating the underlying cause, recurrence is common — around 20-30% within a year for stone-related cases. Cholecystectomy after the first episode, timely stent replacement, and management of PSC or strictures are the main preventive strategies.

faqs.question: What bacteria cause cholangitis?
faqs.answer: The most common organisms are Escherichia coli (25-50% of cases), Klebsiella species (15-20%), Enterococcus (10-15%), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (up to 10% in hospital-acquired cases). Anaerobes such as Bacteroides are less frequent but more common in elderly patients and after previous biliary surgery.

faqs.question: Is cholangitis the same as gallstones?
faqs.answer: No. Gallstones are solid deposits in the gallbladder, very common and often symptomless. Cholangitis is the specific complication that arises when a stone leaves the gallbladder and blocks the common bile duct, allowing bacteria to infect the backed-up bile. You can have gallstones for years without ever developing cholangitis.

faqs.question: What is primary sclerosing cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Primary sclerosing cholangitis (PSC) is a chronic autoimmune disease that causes progressive scarring and narrowing of the bile ducts. It is distinct from acute bacterial cholangitis but puts patients at high risk of repeated cholangitis episodes because structurally abnormal, strictured ducts pool bile. PSC is strongly associated with ulcerative colitis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cholangitis and cholecystitis?
faqs.answer: Cholecystitis is inflammation of the gallbladder wall, usually from a stone blocking the cystic duct. Cholangitis is infection of the bile duct itself. Cholecystitis typically presents without jaundice; cholangitis almost always causes jaundice because the common bile duct is blocked. Both cause right upper abdominal pain and fever.

faqs.question: How long will I be in hospital with cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Most patients with mild-to-moderate cholangitis are discharged within 3-5 days after ERCP and antibiotics. Severe cases requiring intensive care or surgery may need one to three weeks. A planned cholecystectomy may follow as a separate admission 2-6 weeks later.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Empirical first-line choices include piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem for severe cases. Ceftriaxone with metronidazole is used in milder community-acquired cases. Therapy is narrowed once blood and bile cultures identify the organism. The course typically runs 4-7 days after successful biliary drainage.

faqs.question: Can cholangitis cause liver damage?
faqs.answer: A single well-treated episode rarely causes permanent liver damage. Repeated or prolonged obstruction and infection can cause biliary scarring that, over time, may progress to secondary biliary cirrhosis and portal hypertension. This is the main concern in PSC and in patients who have recurrent episodes without addressing the cause.

faqs.question: What is recurrent pyogenic cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Recurrent pyogenic cholangitis is a form predominantly seen in East and Southeast Asia, characterised by repeated infections caused by stones that form within the intrahepatic bile ducts. It is associated with biliary parasites such as Clonorchis sinensis and can lead to liver abscesses and bile duct cancer if not managed over the long term.

faqs.question: Should I avoid fatty foods after cholangitis?
faqs.answer: During recovery, low-fat meals ease biliary stimulation. Long-term, a moderate, balanced fat intake is preferable to very low-fat diets, which can impair gallbladder emptying and encourage stone formation. Large, high-fat meals should be avoided in the first few weeks. Your gastroenterologist will advise on timing of dietary liberalisation.

faqs.question: What is ERCP and is it safe?
faqs.answer: ERCP is an endoscopic procedure that reaches the bile duct opening via the mouth, stomach, and duodenum. It is both diagnostic and therapeutic, removing stones and placing stents. Serious complications affect approximately 3-7% of cases, including pancreatitis, bleeding, and perforation; in the context of treating life-threatening cholangitis, the benefit far outweighs the risk.

faqs.question: Can cholangitis occur without gallstones?
faqs.answer: Yes. Biliary strictures from surgery, cancer, or PSC, indwelling biliary stents that have occluded, and liver-fluke infestation can all cause cholangitis without gallstones. In these cases stone-removal ERCP is not the solution; stenting, stricture dilation, or treating the underlying condition provides drainage.

faqs.question: What are the long-term outcomes after cholangitis?
faqs.answer: Patients whose underlying cause is corrected — most importantly by cholecystectomy after a stone episode — have an excellent long-term prognosis with very low recurrence risk. Those with PSC, malignant obstruction, or recurrent pyogenic cholangitis require ongoing specialist follow-up as their underlying disease determines the trajectory.

metaDescription: Cholangitis is a bile duct bacterial infection causing pain, jaundice and fever. Learn causes, ERCP treatment and prevention. Doctor-reviewed.
